summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier piece about Microtech's problematic Zio driver with a detailed exploration of Mac OS X's filesystem hierarchy, installer etiquette, and kernel panic logging. He explains the distinction between /System/Library/ and /Library/, noting that while third-party software installing in /System/ feels wrong, Apple's own documentation actually recommends it for kernel extensions. He advocates for better installer practices, including full disclosure of what gets installed where and built-in uninstall capabilities. The post also covers the evolution of kernel panic reporting from OS X 10.1's raw console dump to Jaguar's friendlier screen, and notes the ironic limitation that large panic logs fail to get written to disk.
key_points: The /System/Library/ folder is owned by the OS and shouldn't normally be modified by users, while /Library/ is configurable by admins — but Apple's own docs recommend kernel extensions go in /System/Library/Extensions/
Good installer etiquette requires listing everything that will be installed and where, with explanations for privileged locations, plus ideally an uninstall option
Drag-and-drop installation is preferable but not feasible for components requiring privileged filesystem locations
Mac OS X's kernel panic logging has a ~2KB size limit, meaning the most catastrophic panics with the most diagnostic information may fail to log anything at all
money_quotes: Once granted admin privileges, an installer can stick things pretty much anywhere it wants.
Good installer etiquette calls for a complete listing of what will be installed and where, displayed in the readme information before anything actually gets installed.
Sure, most users will ignore it — but higher-functioning users tend to care very much about such things.
And so while Microtech's Zio driver is damnable for numerous reasons, installing itself within /System/ isn't one of them — no matter how hard it is to get it out of there once installed.

Since you cannot take a screen shot in this situation, type the information on another computer or write it out by hand. If you have the ability to photograph the computer screen without a flash, this may be preferred.
The likely reason my Zio panics weren't logged is that the panic generated a large amount of info.
key_insight: Mac OS X's filesystem hierarchy enforces a clear separation between system-owned and user-configurable components, but the practical reality of kernel extensions and installer behavior creates tensions that only good etiquette and transparency can resolve.
